findings.title: websocket_chat function is 510 lines with 6+ nesting levels
findings.code_snippet:
@router.websocket("/ws/chat/{agent_id}")
async def websocket_chat(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    agent_id: uuid.UUID,
    ...
findings.description: The websocket_chat handler at 510 lines is far beyond the 100-line P1 threshold. It handles authentication, session resolution, history loading, OpenClaw routing, quota checks, LLM call orchestration with abort logic, fallback model retry, task creation detection, and message persistence -- all in one function with nesting up to 6 levels deep. Cyclomatic complexity is estimated at 35+.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE: Single 510-line function handling auth, session, routing, LLM calls, abort, fallback, task creation, persistence.
AFTER: Extract into focused helpers:
  1. async def _authenticate_ws(ws, token) -> User
  2. async def _resolve_session(db, agent_id, user_id, session_id) -> str
  3. async def _build_conversation_context(history_messages) -> list[dict]
  4. async def _handle_openclaw_message(ws, agent_id, user_id, conv_id, content)
  5. async def _handle_llm_message(ws, agent, model, conversation, ...)
  6. async def _detect_and_create_task(content, agent_id, user_id)
WHY: Each responsibility becomes independently testable. The main handler becomes a ~50-line orchestrator.

findings.title: call_llm function is 275 lines with complex branching
findings.code_snippet:
async def call_llm(
    model: LLMModel,
    messages: list[dict],
    agent_name: str,
    role_description: str,
    agent_id=None, user_id=None, ...
) -> str:
findings.description: call_llm at 275 lines handles token limit checks, system prompt building, vision format conversion, LLM client creation, tool-calling loop with round warnings, and token tracking. Cyclomatic complexity is estimated at 25+. Note that call_llm_via_engine already exists as a cleaner alternative gated behind a feature flag -- this legacy path duplicates much of that logic.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE: 275-line call_llm with inline vision processing, tool loop, token tracking.
AFTER: 1) Extract _convert_vision_messages(api_messages, supports_vision) -> list[LLMMessage]
  2) Extract _run_tool_loop(client, api_messages, tools, max_rounds, callbacks) -> str
  3) Deprecate call_llm once execution_engine_v2 flag is fully rolled out.
WHY: The execution engine (engine.py) already implements the same loop more cleanly via middleware. Dual maintenance is a liability.

findings.title: create_agent handler is 172 lines with 4-level nesting
findings.description: The create_agent endpoint at 172 lines handles quota checking, tenant resolution, agent construction, lifecycle transition, participant creation, permissions, OpenClaw API key generation, file system initialization, skill copying, container starting, and audit logging. Function > 100 lines triggers P1.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE: 172-line create_agent handler mixing orchestration with file I/O and container management.
AFTER: Extract:
  1. _resolve_tenant_defaults(db, target_tenant_id) -> dict with max_llm_calls, etc.
  2. _setup_agent_permissions(db, agent, data, current_user)
  3. _copy_skills_to_agent(db, agent_id, skill_ids, agent_dir)
  4. _setup_openclaw_agent(db, agent, current_user) -> dict
The handler becomes a ~50-line orchestrator calling these helpers.
WHY: Reduces cognitive load, enables unit testing of each concern independently.

findings.title: Agent model uses raw strings for status, agent_type, security_zone
findings.code_snippet:
agent_type: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(20), default="native")
agent_class: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(30), default="general")
security_zone: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(30), default="standard")
status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Enum("creating", ..., create_constraint=False))
findings.description: Encapsulation: 3/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.0). The Agent model uses plain strings for agent_type ('native'|'openclaw'), agent_class ('general'|'specialist'|'executive'), security_zone ('public'|'standard'|'restricted'), and status. While status uses an Enum column with create_constraint=False (defeating the purpose), agent_type/class/zone have no type-level constraints. The schema layer (AgentCreate) also uses raw strings with only comment-based documentation of valid values.
findings.suggestion:
Define Python enums and use them in both the model and schema:

class AgentType(str, Enum):
    NATIVE = "native"
    OPENCLAW = "openclaw"

class SecurityZone(str, Enum):
    PUBLIC = "public"
    STANDARD = "standard"
    RESTRICTED = "restricted"

Then use: agent_type: AgentType = Field(default=AgentType.NATIVE) in schemas, and Mapped[AgentType] in the model. This makes illegal values unrepresentable at both the API boundary and the database layer.

findings.title: Agent model has 40+ columns but zero domain behavior methods
findings.code_snippet:
class Agent(Base):
    __tablename__ = "agents"
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = ...
    name: Mapped[str] = ...
    # ... 40+ columns, zero methods
findings.description: Encapsulation: 2/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 3.5). The Agent model is a pure data container with 40+ columns and zero methods. Business logic like token counter resets (_lazy_reset_token_counters in agents.py), expiry checks (is_agent_expired in permissions.py), and lifecycle transitions (transition() called externally) are scattered across API handlers and utility functions. This is a textbook anemic domain model.
findings.suggestion:
Move domain behavior into the Agent model:

class Agent(Base):
    def reset_token_counters_if_needed(self) -> bool: ...
    def is_expired(self) -> bool: ...
    def transition_to(self, target_status: str, ctx: TransitionContext): ...
    def can_accept_messages(self) -> bool: ...
    @property
    def is_openclaw(self) -> bool: return self.agent_type == 'openclaw'

This co-locates invariants with the data they protect, making it harder to bypass validation.

findings.title: update_agent_permissions accepts untyped dict body
findings.code_snippet:
@router.put("/{agent_id}/permissions")
async def update_agent_permissions(
    agent_id: uuid.UUID,
    data: dict,
    ...
):
    scope_type = data.get("scope_type", "company")
    scope_ids = data.get("scope_ids", [])
findings.description: The update_agent_permissions endpoint accepts a raw dict instead of a Pydantic schema. No validation occurs on scope_type values, scope_ids format, or access_level. An invalid scope_type silently passes through. Similarly, resolve_agent_approval (line 708) also uses raw dict. This bypasses FastAPI's automatic request validation.
findings.suggestion:
Define a Pydantic model:

class AgentPermissionUpdate(BaseModel):
    scope_type: Literal['company', 'department', 'user']
    scope_ids: list[uuid.UUID] = []
    access_level: Literal['use', 'manage'] = 'use'

Then use: data: AgentPermissionUpdate in the handler signature. FastAPI will reject invalid input before your code runs.

findings.title: Duplicate lazy token counter reset loop in list_agents
findings.description: The exact same 6-line token reset loop appears twice in list_agents (once for admin path, once for non-admin path). This is a near-duplicate code block that should be deduplicated.
findings.suggestion:
BEFORE: Two identical 6-line blocks at lines 88-94 and 128-133.
AFTER: Extract a helper:
  async def _reset_counters_for_agents(agents: list[Agent], db: AsyncSession):
      if any(await _lazy_reset_token_counters(a, db) for a in agents):
          await db.commit()
Then call it once after each query path.
WHY: Eliminates copy-paste; single place to modify if reset logic changes.

findings.title: Frontend API service uses 'any' type extensively
findings.code_snippet:
create: (data: any) =>
    request<any>('/agents/', ...),
metrics: (id: string) =>
    request<any>(`/agents/${id}/metrics`),
collaborators: (id: string) =>
    request<any[]>(`/agents/${id}/collaborators`),
templates: () =>
    request<any[]>('/agents/templates'),
findings.description: The agentApi object uses 'any' for create input, metrics response, collaborators response, templates response, and gateway messages. Similarly, taskApi.create, channelApi.create/update, scheduleApi, skillApi, toolApi, and triggerApi all use 'any' types. This defeats TypeScript's type safety and provides zero IDE assistance at call sites.

findings.title: EnterpriseSettings duplicates the fetch wrapper from api.ts
findings.description: EnterpriseSettings.tsx defines its own fetchJson<T> helper (lines 12-24) that duplicates the request<T> function from api.ts. It differs in URL prefix ('/api' vs '/api/v1') and lacks the auto-logout on 401 and Pydantic error formatting. Some calls in this file use fetchJson while others use the imported api service functions, creating an inconsistent data fetching layer.
findings.suggestion: Remove the local fetchJson and route all calls through the centralized api.ts service. For endpoints that need '/api' prefix instead of '/api/v1', add them to the appropriate api object in api.ts. This ensures consistent auth handling, error formatting, and 401 auto-logout across all calls.

findings.title: ExecutionState and middleware protocol are well-designed
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 7/10, Usefulness: 8/10, Enforcement: 6/10 (Aggregate: 7.0). The execution engine demonstrates good design: ExecutionState is a focused dataclass with typed fields; AgentMiddleware is a Protocol with optional hooks (return None to no-op); the engine composes middleware in forward/reverse order correctly. The cancelled event for cooperative cancellation is a solid pattern. Minor weakness: no validation that agent_id/tenant_id are valid at construction time.
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a __post_init__ to ExecutionState to validate that agent_id is not None and max_rounds > 0. This would raise Enforcement from 6 to 8. Overall this is the strongest type design in the reviewed files.

positive_observations: The execution engine (engine.py) uses a well-structured middleware pattern with Protocol typing, composable hooks, and cooperative cancellation via asyncio.Event -- a significant architectural improvement over the monolithic call_llm.
The agent lifecycle uses a dedicated state machine (domain/agent_lifecycle.py) with TransitionContext, properly separating domain rules from the API layer.
CollaborationService has a clean fallback pattern: Redis Streams event bus with file-based inbox fallback, ensuring message delivery even when Redis is down.
Feature flags are cleanly implemented with proper Redis cache invalidation and admin-only access control.
